title-group.article-title: An empirical investigation of environmental impacts of agglomeration economies in major cities of Pakistan

contrib-group.aff: 1https://ror.org/02bf6br77grid.444924.b0000 0004 0608 7936Department of Economics, Lahore College for Women University, Lahore, Pakistan
2https://ror.org/040gec961grid.411555.10000 0001 2233 7083Sustainable Development Study Centre, Government College University, Lahore, Pakistan

license.license-p: Open Access This article is licensed under a Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial-NoDerivatives 4.0 International License, which permits any non-commercial use, sharing, distribution and reproduction in any medium or format, as long as you give appropriate credit to the original author(s) and the source, provide a link to the Creative Commons licence, and indicate if you modified the licensed material. You do not have permission under this licence to share adapted material derived from this article or parts of it. The images or other third party material in this article are included in the article’s Creative Commons licence, unless indicated otherwise in a credit line to the material. If material is not included in the article’s Creative Commons licence and your intended use is not permitted by statutory regulation or exceeds the permitted use, you will need to obtain permission directly from the copyright holder. To view a copy of this licence, visit http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-nd/4.0/.

abstract.p: Agglomeration economies arise when firms and people co-locate in cities. These economies are determined by city’s activity, size, land and labor prices, and affect the overall environmental conditions of cities in developing economies. The present study empirically investigates environmental impacts of agglomeration economies in major cities of Pakistan during 2002–2019, using Dynamic Fixed Effects (DFE), Pooled Mean Group (PMG), and Mean Group (MG). The findings of the MG model reveal that over the time, the physical environment is negatively affected by agglomeration economies; e.g. population increase (−1.89, p < 0.05), increase in number of factories (−7.11, p < 0.001), increased number of registered vehicles (−1.02, p < 0.05), by creating pressures on human health, civic amenities, and air quality of cities. The study suggests decentralization of big cities, and reshaping of medium and small cities to control population pressure and industrial pollution in major cities of Pakistan.

sec.title: Supplementary Information
sec.p: The online version contains supplementary material available at 10.1038/s41598-025-03871-2.

sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: Globally, the structural transformation, urbanization, rural-urban migration, and reclassification of rural areas to urban settings are increasing at an unprecedented level9. The advantages that result from the spatial concentration of people (urbanized economies) and industry (localized economies) in an urban region are known as agglomeration economies16,28. Large labor pool and co-location of enterprises for input supplies are examples of supply-side agglomeration externalities40,41. These agglomeration benefits are countered by cities’ unchecked population growth and industrial expansion, which leads to agglomeration diseconomies in terms of different socioeconomic challenges, e.g., urban poverty, crime, traffic congestion, road accidents, pollution, waste management, disease prevalence, and real estate competition1,17,21,26,27,59. Estimates suggest that world’s urban population will increase by 2.5 billion people in 205054. It is expected that above 60% of this increase will occur in Asia. Similarly, South Asian urban population is expected to increase up to 250 million by 203056. This rise would be obvious predominantly in China, India, Pakistan, Bangladesh, the Philippines, and Vietnam, in next three decades54. The rising urban agglomerations with concentration of population and industry in cities, and changing urban lifestyle have brought about various environmental challenges9,25,28–30. These include poor urban air quality (from domestic, industrial and vehicular exhaust), water pollution (domestic and industrial discharges), solid waste generation, rising carbon emissions, and increase in energy consumption5,15,23. These environmental hazards severely affect human health and overall quality of life61. According to World Health Organization [WHO] estimates, around 90% of low and middle-income countries have limited access to clean air57.

The quality of physical environment of cities is generally measured using air quality indices. Although the magnitude and characteristics of air pollutants vary across cities depend upon the industrial, domestic and other activities, accompanying various health hazards50. In the frame of agglomeration economies, air pollution is caused by concentrated sources such as transport and industries, in the cities14,52,53. The long-term exposure to air pollutants (e.g. PM2.5, NO2, and O3) leads to adverse health impacts that reduce efficiency and productivity. According to estimates, air pollution costs the world, 8 billion dollars a day18,19,58.

Although, the level of air pollution, urbanization, size of the exposed population, fuel type and other supply infrastructure, and the resources and institutions available to carry out air quality management are different in South Asian cities. Many prominent South Asian cities regularly surpass the ambient air quality standards of WHO39. Transportation, industry, and construction activities are among the major urban sources of pollution6. Particulate matter (PM), tropospheric ozone (O3), carbon monoxide (CO), nitrogen oxides (NOx), sulfur oxides (SOx) and volatile organic compounds (VOCs) are some of the major air pollutants that cause negative health and environmental impacts33,46,47,49. Pakistan has different monitoring stations located in Islamabad, Lahore, Karachi Quetta, and Peshawar that measure PM104,6,48. The inhalation of PM10 affects lungs and consequently affects the upper respiratory tract that is also connected with premature deaths4,6. Despite strong scientific evidence PM2.5 is the most dangerous subset of PM10 and penetrates deeply in the respiratory tract4. However, limited systematic and regular monitoring information on PM2.5 is available in Pakistan39. Sulfur dioxide (SO2) is a by-product of burning fossil fuels, which transforms it into sulfates that ultimately appear as fine particles. NOx derives from vehicular exhaust, combustion and installation of power and industrial plants and agricultural activity. NOx reacts with other air pollutants to form O3, fine particulates (nitrates) and smog in the lower atmosphere, and cause damages to health ranging from eye irritation to decreased lung capacity. Whereas CO is a product of incomplete fuels combustion, causing headaches, dizziness, disorientation, nausea, and fatigue33,46,47,49,56.

Pakistan is the South Asia’s most urbanized economy with more than 80 million people living in cities21,22. It is anticipated that urbanization would expand even more quickly as the percentage of industry and commerce increases40,41. Urban population is growing at the rate of 3.3% annually as a result of migration and structural transformation21. Nonetheless, big cities contribute 55% of the country’s total gross domestic product (GDP). In addition, Pakistan collects 95% of its total federal tax revenue from ten most populous cities i.e. Karachi, Lahore, Faisalabad, Rawalpindi, Gujranwala, Multan, Peshawar, Hyderabad, Quetta and Islamabad. Because of this growth, more individuals and businesses are drawn to settle in the major cities21,22, in anticipation of associated higher wages and benefits respectively2. Therefore, the size of the cities is rapidly expanding. Presently, ten Pakistani cities are declared urban agglomerations (Table SI-1), which generally exceed the permissible air quality standards of WHO (World Health Organization)3,6,39,48.

In Pakistan, presently, limited empirical literature is found on environmental impacts of agglomerations economies. Few studies investigated the impacts of agglomeration economies on industrial growth and their impact on economy of Pakistan. Different studies show that quality of environment has been deteriorated with the rise in agglomerations, especially in developing economies7,25. However, the environmental impacts of agglomeration economies in Pakistan are not much studied. On the other side, the studies on air quality of the cities of Pakistan mostly focus on the measurement of different parameters of air quality using primary data (cross-sectional studies), and compare these data with the national and international air quality levels4,6,8,37,48. Very few studies are found which have analyzed the long run environmental impacts of air pollutants36,45. More specifically, the long run impact of agglomeration economies on physical environment (air quality) is not much explored for Pakistani cities. Therefore, to fill this gap in empirical literature, this study aimed to analyze the short-run and long-run environmental impacts of agglomeration economies, using a Panel data of eighteen years (2002–2019) for nine major cities in Pakistan.

sec.title: Model specification
sec.p: The Eq. (1) describes the general form of the relationship; the dependent variable of environment (ENV) depends on different factors of agglomerations economies. The Eq. (2) is the econometric form of the Eq. (1). Further, the dependent variable (ENV) is determined by Air Quality Index (AQI), developed using PCA method. AQI is composed of factors of PM2.5, PM10, SO2, NO2, CO, and O3. The Eq. (3) describes the relationship of AQI and all selected factors of agglomeration economies (i.e. population size (POG), number of firms (NF), number of employees (NE), housing availability (HO), and number of vehicles (VEH)). The Eq. (3) of the model depicts the agglomeration economies affect the air quality of cities. The notation in subscript i represents cross-sections (cities), and t represents time period (2002–2019), in the panel data equations.

sec.title: Data and sources
sec.p: The present study used secondary data sources to analyze the impacts of Agglomeration Economies on Air Quality of the major cities of Pakistan. The air quality data is collected from air quality reports of Environment Protection and Climate Change Department (EP&CCD), and Compendium of Environment, Pakistan Bureau of Statistics (PBS). The EP&CCD data is available on daily basis. Therefore, initially the data is converted to yearly basis to generate a longitudinal series. Other data from compendium of environment and World Bank statistics was already available on yearly basis. The data on variables of agglomeration economies was collected from annual published reports of provincial Development Statistics, industrial reports, and World Bank statistics. It is noteworthy that the official statistics for other provincial capital cities like Karachi, Peshawar, and Quetta, were not available online prior to year 2002. Therefore the study limited the time-frame of data from year 2002 to 2019. At the time of data analysis of this research, all of these data was available up till year 2019.

For the present study, nine (09) major cities of Pakistan; Karachi, Lahore, Faisalabad, Rawalpindi, Gujranwala, Multan, Peshawar, Hyderabad, and Quetta, respectively were selected as the study sample. All these cities were selected with a minimum population of one million which satisfies the criteria of being a large city, and being an urban agglomeration (Table SI-1). Karachi, capital city of Sindh province, is the most populous city of the country. It is the most vital port, and the largest stock exchange of Pakistan, and headquarters of major corporations. Lahore, the provincial capital of Punjab, is the second largest city of Pakistan, a hub of trade, industry, transportation, politics, education, and entertainment. Faisalabad is recognized for its industrial growth, chemical industry, various textile mills, and fertilizer manufacturing plants. Rawalpindi is a rapidly growing city in the Potohar region of Punjab. Gujranwala is an industrial and commercial center, playing an important role in supporting the economy. Multan, in the southern Punjab, is a good agricultural market25. Peshawar city is the provincial capital of KP province, and has tremendous historical, political, and economic importance. Hyderabad city of Sindh province, 150 km from Karachi, has agricultural and industrial importance. Quetta is the provincial capital of Balochistan, known for its diversity of plants and wildlife. The central capital city, Islamabad, which is also one of the agglomerated cities of Pakistan, is excluded from the empirical analysis due to limited data on environmental and agglomeration variables. All these cities are called urban agglomerations now. Map depicting the major cities of Pakistan24 is provided in Figure SI-1. Along with positive agglomeration economies, the cities are facing challenges of negative economies in the form of poor air quality3. The city-wise long-run trends (based on a panel data of nine cities and eighteen years) of agglomeration economies and air quality are presented in Fig. 1.

sec.title: Methodology
sec.p: Application of PMG (Pooled Mean Group), MG (Mean Group), and DFE (Dynamic Fixed Effect) for variables with varying orders of integration is an important characteristic of panel data techniques44. Measurement of long-run relationship among variables is possible despite varying order of integration. Through pooled mean group (PMG) estimation, the regression generates estimators that are consistent regardless of whether the variables of the model are integrated of order zero [I (0)], or integrated of order one, [I (1)]42–44. It Permits the short-run slope parameters to vary over cities. However, PMG estimator is not applicable if any of the model’s variables are integrated of order two, [I (2)]42. The Mean Group (MG) permits both long-run and short-run slope parameters to vary over cities43. MG is used in the same conditions as PMG. Nonetheless, it is used for comparison purposes also. The Dynamic Fixed Effects model (DFE) doesn’t permit either long-run or short-run slope parameters to vary over cities. It includes a unique intercept for each cross-section (city). DFE provides cluster option for intra-group comparisons10. It is used to compare with PMG. The study applied; the Pooled Mean Group (PMG), the Mean Group (MG), and the Dynamic Fixed Effects (DFE) techniques of panel data estimation, to test both the short-run and long-run relationship of air quality index with the variables of agglomeration economies. Besides, the selection of appropriate technique among the three is based on Hausman Test.
The lagged model is presented in Eq. 5, given below:

sec.title: Results and discussion
sec.p: The findings of the study are presented in graphical analysis, descriptive analysis, and model estimation, respectively.

sec.title: Graphical analysis
sec.p: The graphical analysis depicting the year wise trends in variables of agglomeration economies is collectively provided in Fig. 1. It exhibits upward growth in population of mega cities of Karachi and Lahore, over the years. Moreover, Karachi, Lahore, Faisalabad, and Gujranwala cities depict an upward rise in the number of registered firms, in the more recent years. The graphs of Rawalpindi, Peshawar and Multan show a slight rise in the number of firms in previous ten years. Cities of Karachi, Lahore, Faisalabad, Gujranwala, and Peshawar show the rise in number of employees in the firms. People living in rented homes are another determinant of urban agglomerations. Karachi, Lahore, Rawalpindi, Gujranwala, Peshawar, Hyderabad, and Quetta show an upward rise in the number of people living in rented accommodations. The city-wise number of registered vehicles showed a falling trend in the number of vehicles in Karachi city in recent years. Lahore is the only city that showed upward rise in number of vehicles throughout the study period. Lastly air quality index was based on particulate matter (PM2.5 and PM10), Sulfur Dioxide (SO2), Nitrogen Dioxide (NO2), Carbon Monoxide (CO), and Ozone (O3). The environment (AQI) is affected by the population and industrial agglomeration economies occurring in the cities. The graph of Karachi city shows a positive trend in the air quality index. Similarly, the air quality index for the city of Multan is rising upward showing an improved air quality. Lahore, Faisalabad, Rawalpindi, and Gujranwala, Peshawar and Quetta show poor air quality. It may represent industrial and vehicular emissions in the cities. The trends in air quality also support the forecast of air pollutants55, provided in Table SI-3.

sec.title: Descriptive statistics
sec.p: The study developed the air quality index using principal component analysis (PCA), for the panel data of nine cities and 18 years (2002–2019), following rules of retaining the number of components based on eigenvalues34. The details of variables used in development of air quality index are available in Table SI-2.

The summary statistics for all independent and dependent variables are provided in Table 1. It depicts 162 observations for a panel of 09 cities for 18 years (2002–2019). The average value of POG is 3.92%, with 3.99% deviation from average. It ranges from a minimum of 0.64–15.74%. The average number of registered firms (NF) is 903.91, with a standard deviation of 791.97. The indicator representing employment is the number of employees in the firms (NE), and has an average value of 82129.08 employees at all levels of employment (primary, secondary, and tertiary). The average value of housing availability (HO) is 15.27%. The average number of registered motorized vehicles is 739660.7, in these cities.

The physical environmental conditions are measured using air quality indicators (PMs, SO2, NO2, CO and O3). The particulate matter-PM2.5 has an annual average value of 143.87 µg/m3, exceeding the 24 h-standard levels of 35 µg/m3, deviation from average is calculated as 102.80. The values of PM2.5 range from 28 to 728 micron per cubic meter (µg/m3). The PM10 has an annual average value 193.03 µg/m3, exceeding the 24 h-standard levels of 150 µg/m3. The standard deviation value is 78.41. The average value of sulfur dioxide (SO2) is 70.99 µg/m3, which lies within the 24 h-standard of 120 µg/m3. The annual average value of Nitrogen Dioxide (NO2) appears 64.28 µg/m3, and lies within the 24 h-standard limit of 80 µg/m3. The annual average value of Carbon Monoxide (CO) lies at 4.32 µg/m3, within the 1 h-standard limit of 5 µg/m3. The mean value of Ozone (O3) appears 65.99 µg/m3, lies within the 8 h-standard limit of 130 µg/m3.

Correlation coefficients of the variables are given in Table 2. The results exhibit a low to moderate degree of association between all independent variables which indicates no visible sign of multicollinearity in the model. There is also a moderate correlation among independent and dependent variables. All independent variables show a positive correlation among them. The AQI shows a negative correlation with number of firms (NF) and number of registered vehicles (VEH) in the cities, as was expected. However, it shows a positive correlated with other independent variables.

The diagnostic tests comprising cross-sectional dependency test, Wooldridge test of autocorrelation and Wald test of Heteroscedasticity are illustrated in Table 3. The output of CD test shows that all cross-sectional entities (09 cities) are independent of socioeconomic, cultural and infrastructural conditions. CD test accepts the null hypothesis of independent cross-sections. The output of Wooldridge for panel data is a robust test, showing that no evidence of serial correlation exists in the data. Besides, the data is suitable for testing the econometric models. Moreover, Wald test for Heteroskedasticity confirms that all entities (09 cities) in panel data are not homogenous.

Additionally, the data series is pre-tested using unit root tests (Levin-Lin-Chu, Im-Pesaran-Shin, and Augmented Dicky Fuller)32. For a detailed analysis of the data series, all variables are tested without trend (WOT) and with trend (WT), and both at level and at first difference. The null hypothesis for unit root test is that the data series contains a unit root. The alternate hypothesis is that the data series is stationary. It is found that variables are integrated of mixed order; [I (0)] and [I (1)]32. Table 4 provides the results of unit root for all proxy variables and air quality index, suggesting the suitability of models of Pooled Mean Group (PMG), Mean Group (MG), and Dynamic Fixed Effects (DFE) for the data under this study.

PMG, MG and DFE estimators, with maximum lag of one, are selected on the basis of Schwartz Bayesian Criterion (SBC) to estimate the long-run and short-run impacts of agglomeration economies on environmental conditions in the major cities of Pakistan. Initially, the air quality-index is developed using principal component analysis (PCA) technique (for details, see Table SI-2). Therefore, the study has estimated the impact of agglomeration economies on the air quality of the cities, using the Eqs. (1–6) respectively.

caption.p: Graphical analysis showing environmental impacts of agglomerations.
Unit root test of independent variables (Aes) and dependent variables (air quality index).

table-wrap-foot.p: Note: The probability values are provided in parentheses.
WOT without trend, WT with trend.

caption.p: Estimates of pooled mean group (PMG), mean group (MG) and dynamic fixed effects (DFE) models.

td.p: Ho: PMG is preferred than MG
Ho: DFE is preferred than PMG
Ho: The DFE is preferred than MG

table-wrap-foot.p: Note: Total no. of observations is 153, and no. of groups is 9, for all equations.
The values in parentheses are the standard-errors.

sec.title: Model estimation
sec.p: Table 5 represents the PMG, MG and DFE estimates of the Air Quality Index. Based on the Hausman test results, Mean Group (MG) is preferred over PMG and DFE for Air Quality index. Therefore, the estimates of MG are only discussed. The index is comprised of six indicators i.e. Particulate matter (PM10 and PM2.5), Sulfur Dioxide (SO2), Nitrogen Oxide (NO2), Ozone (O3), and Carbon Monoxide (CO). The significant negative value of ECT (83%) shows that the deviation from log-run equilibrium is corrected in the model, in one year. The results show that population growth has a significant negative impact on the air quality index (at 5%) in the long-run. The rise in the number of factories has a significant negative impact (at 1%) on the air quality of the cities of Pakistan. The industries using fossil fuels emit significant amounts of air pollutants. The sulfur emissions from large-scale industries are a major contributor to poor air quality56. However, apart from industrial sources of air emissions, agriculture sources also affect the environment. For example, the burning of solid waste and sugarcane fields produce carbon monoxide (CO) and particulate matter (PM10 and PM2.5) in major cities of Pakistan, e.g. Islamabad, Lahore, Karachi, Peshawar and Quetta45,56. Their concentration levels exceed the National Environmental Quality Standards [NEQS]1.

The employment in factories has also shown a significant negative impact on the air quality index, in the long-run. However, the coefficient of housing has not any significant impact on the physical environment of the cities, for short-run and long-run. The increase in vehicle traffic has a considerable detrimental on the air quality index, in the long-run. The rapid motorization and increased energy consumption have a negative impact on the quality of air of Pakistani cities56. The previous studies show that the high concentration of particulate matter (PM2.5) raised the prevalence of cardiac diseases in people of Karachi city38. Both PM10 and PM2.5 cause significant health problems, including premature mortality, chronic respiratory disease, aggravated asthma, acute respiratory symptoms, and decreased lung function4,6,8,37,48,55. The emerging big city, Faisalabad is also facing the environmental pollution of coarse particles (PM10) and fine particles (PM2.5) with the rise in population, industrial and agricultural activities9. The environment has been severely polluted by rapid expansion of cities in the surrounding countries too. China’s central government has recently implemented a number of measures to reduce pollution, including more stringent regulations on major pollutants30,61, increased funds for the treatment of environmental pollution60 and the adoption of the toughest environment law ever. The quality of the ecological environment in many places of China has not improved despite the great commitment of the central government to combat pollution12. Environmental challenges like haze and declining water quality continue to pose a serious threat to public health and obstruct the nation’s sustainable development51. Nonetheless, ICT and education plays important role in improving environmental quality and creating a sustainable future35.

sec.title: Conclusion and recommendations
sec.p: The present study empirically investigates the environmental impacts of agglomeration economies in the major cities (i.e. Karachi, Lahore, Faisalabad, Gujranwala, Rawalpindi, Peshawar, Multan, Hyderabad, and Quetta) of Pakistan. The data was collected for the previous 18 years from 2002 to 2019, from different published sources, to check the long run impacts of agglomeration economies on environmental conditions of cities in Pakistan. The validity and suitability of data for econometric modeling was checked using different diagnostic tests, graphical analysis, by taking descriptive statistics, correlation coefficients, and unit root tests. Based on the Hausman tests, the study selected the most suitable model among DFE, PMG and MG. The findings of this research depict that the concentration of population and industries, along with creating positive economies of improved living standards, also creates environmental challenge of poor air quality for the major cities of Pakistan. The results clearly conclude that unbridled population pressures towards cities, and uncontrolled and non-progressive growth in number of factories and rising number of vehicles are creating negative externalities for cities.

The study suggests taking measures to control the unbridled urbanization through integrated and sustainable initiatives. Measures to internalize the negative externalities; generated by polluting industries and rising number of vehicles, are also needed to apply by effective tax implementation. Imposition of installing emission control systems for polluting industries, fitness certificates for vehicles, car-pooling may also be persuaded. Cheaper and environment friendly alternative public transport services also play significant role in improving environmental quality of cities. Nevertheless, decentralization of large cities and development of secondary cities can be an effective urban management tool. Provision of administrative services is also suggested for small towns and villages to control the out-migration, towards big cities. The study suggests further research on decentralization of services in cities. While most of the data was not available on cities level, the study suggests a cross-sectional type research to compare the large, medium and small cities and identify the socio-economic and environmental challenges and opportunities among them. It is further suggested to compare and follow the models of developed economies to overcome the emerging multiple challenges of urban agglomerations and opt for sustainable urban development in cities of Pakistan. By implementing such measures, cities can harness benefits of agglomeration economies, while minimizing the negative environmental impacts, and developing sustainable urban environment.

sec.title: Electronic supplementary material
sec.p: Below is the link to the electronic supplementary material.

fn.p: Particulate Matter (PM10) at 150/hr, Particulate Matter (PM2.5) at 35/24 hr, Nitrogen Oxide (NO2) at 80/24 hr, Sulfur Dioxide (SO2) at 120/24 hr, Carbon Monoxide (CO) at 5/8 hr, and Ozone (O3) at 130/hr. All these pollutants are measured at Microgram per cubic meter (ug/m3).
Springer Nature remains neutral with regard to jurisdictional claims in published maps and institutional affiliations.

notes.title: Author contributions
notes.p: N.G. conceived the idea, conducted study, analyzed data and wrote this manuscript. M.N. validated the analysis and helped in interpreting the results. G.Z.G. proof read the draft and provided technical guidance. F.S. provided technical support.

notes.title: Funding
notes.p: This research did not receive any specific grant from funding agencies in the public, commercial, or not-for profit sectors.

notes.title: Data availability
notes.p: The data generated and analyzed in this study is included in this article and in supplementary material.

notes.title: Competing interests
notes.p: The authors declare no competing interests.
